feedingProfile.preferredNpk: higher-phosphorus houseplant fertilizer (e.g. 10-15-10) diluted to half strength, growing season only

commonIssues: Root rot from overwatering or poor drainage — the number-one killer; let soil dry completely between waterings and use a pot with drainage holes
Reduce watering to a minimum in winter (dormant period); soil should be barely moist
Sunburn when moved from low light to bright direct sun too quickly — transition gradually
Not frost tolerant; bring indoors before night temperatures dip below 10C (50F)
Outdoor summer plants should be protected from rain to avoid waterlogged soil
An oversized pot holds excess moisture and promotes root rot — pot up only 2.5-4 cm (1-1.5 in) wider than the root ball
Mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and horses if ingested
Indoor plants rarely flower; mature plants (3+ years) in bright light may bloom

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Aloe vera: water very moderately spring–fall, minimum watering in winter; full sun, adapts to part shade; winter minimum 40F (4C); bring indoors before nights drop below 50F; protect outdoor plants from rain; hardy zones 10-12; sandy well-drained potting loam
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Aloe vera: winter minimum 40F (~4C); not frost hardy below zone 10; bring indoors before night temps dip below 50F

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Aloe vera: full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) to partial shade; allow soil to completely dry between waterings; water less in winter; very well-drained succulent soil; clay pot with several drainage holes; zones 10a-12b; rosette habit spreading by offsets; mealybugs, aphids, scale; toxic to cats/dogs/horses (low severity)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Aloe vera: allow soil to completely dry between waterings; overwatering causes root rot; clay pot with drainage holes recommended

Penn State Extension, 'Aloe, a Hardy Houseplant' — water thoroughly when soil surface is dry during growing season (spring–early fall); keep barely moist during fall–winter dormancy; err on the side of underwatering (root-rot prone); repot every ~2 years into a pot only 1-1.5 in larger than the root ball; coarse cactus/succulent mix; not frost tolerant

UF/IFAS Gardening Solutions — Aloe Vera: water only when top inch of soil is dry, water well, never let plant sit in water; do not increase pot by more than two sizes (oversized pots hold excess moisture and cause root rot); zones 8-11 outdoors; sunburn when moved to bright light too quickly

University of Minnesota Extension, 'Cacti and succulents' — allow soil to dry completely between waterings; water minimally in winter; fertilize succulents three to four times during the brighter months with a half-strength houseplant fertilizer higher in phosphorus than nitrogen; bright sunny window; pots must have drainage holes; one part potting soil to one part coarse sand

Confidence note: extension sources give moisture-based rules ('water when soil is completely dry'), not fixed day counts; baseIntervalDays=14 is a conservative planner estimate of that drying cycle for a typical indoor pot. feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays=60 is derived from UMN's '3-4 times during the brighter months'. heatStressC=38 and container liters (3-10 L ≈ 15-25 cm pots, per PSU/UF pot-sizing guidance for a 1-2 ft rosette) are reasoned conversions, not directly published figures.
